title: Pin<Box<ClientInner>> with explicit field-drop-order comment is correct
description: Client stores all C state in a Pin<Box<ClientInner>> with fields declared in a specific order: client_ctx, then comm_cfg, then transport. The comment above ClientInner explains that Rust drops fields in declaration order and that wh_Client_Cleanup (called in Drop for Client before any fields drop) needs comm_cfg alive, and comm_cfg needs transport alive. Why this is correct: the alternative — storing raw pointers and relying on manual ordering — is more error-prone. The Pin ensures the heap address of client_ctx never changes after wh_Client_Init, which is required because wolfCrypt's CryptoCb system stores the raw pointer. A future contributor might: (1) reorder the fields for aesthetic reasons, breaking the drop invariant silently; (2) suggest removing Pin because 'Box already heap-allocates', missing that Pin also prevents moving out of the Box through safe code; (3) suggest splitting ClientInner into separate allocations, requiring careful lifetime management. The current design should be preserved and the comment should not be deleted.
